title: TaskPaper vs OmniFocus for Minimalists
meta_description: TaskPaper vs OmniFocus for minimalists who want plain text task lists without perspectives, review modes, or layered systems.
constraint_lens: You want plain text task lists without layered systems like perspectives, review modes, or complex project views.

verdict.summary: TaskPaper wins for minimalists who prefer plain text task lists. It uses a simple outline format with projects and indented tasks. OmniFocus includes perspectives, review modes, and context filters that add conceptual layers. If advanced perspectives and review modes introduce extra conceptual layers, OmniFocus fails first.
verdict.decision_rule: If advanced perspectives and review modes introduce extra conceptual layers, OmniFocus fails first.

sections.heading: Best fit for text-first task management
sections.content: You want simple hierarchical tasks without learning a system. TaskPaper works as a plain text outline where indentation defines structure. OmniFocus organizes tasks through projects, tags, perspectives, and review workflows that require understanding multiple views.

bullets.why_it_matters: You write tasks as indented lines without switching between views or dashboards.

bullets.point: Minimal interface with no forced perspectives
bullets.why_it_matters: You see your list as written rather than navigating filtered task views.
You add simple tags inline instead of configuring separate property panels.
You create tailored task views, though this requires understanding filtering rules.
You systematically revisit projects, adding structure beyond a simple list.

bullets.point: Forecast view combining due dates and calendar events
bullets.why_it_matters: You see tasks and calendar items together, but must manage more moving parts.

sections.heading: Where each tool can break down

items.fails_when: You need filtered dashboards and structured review cycles across many projects.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use OmniFocus if advanced filtering and review workflows become necessary.

items.fails_when: You feel overwhelmed by perspectives, review prompts, or multiple task views.
items.what_to_do_instead: Switch to TaskPaper for a single plain text list.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: If your workload grows large and you need filtered views to focus on specific contexts, OmniFocus may become helpful despite its complexity.

sections.heading: Quick rules
rules: If you want plain text hierarchical lists, choose TaskPaper.
If layered perspectives feel like overkill, avoid OmniFocus.
If advanced filtering becomes essential, consider OmniFocus.

faqs.a: It supports basic tag-based searches, but not layered perspective systems.
It includes perspectives, review modes, and forecast views that add conceptual layers.

faqs.q: Which is better for minimal task tracking?
faqs.a: TaskPaper is better for a simple text-first workflow.

faqs.q: Which has the higher ceiling for structured task management?
faqs.a: OmniFocus has a higher ceiling due to advanced filtering and review features.
